Arguments.prompt: Download US state boundary shapefiles from https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/GENZ2018/shp/cb_2018_us_state_20m.zip and US county boundary shapefiles from https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/GENZ2018/shp/cb_2018_us_county_5m.zip. Unzip both archives. Load the state shapefile and filter to the single feature where the NAME field equals 'Florida'. Load the county shapefile and filter to the single feature where the NAME field equals 'Miami-Dade' and the STATEFP field matches Florida's STATEFP (which is '12'). Compute the bounding box of Miami-Dade County by taking its geometry's minx, miny, maxx, maxy values, then expand each side by 0.5 degrees: expanded_minx = minx - 0.5, expanded_miny = miny - 0.5, expanded_maxx = maxx + 0.5, expanded_maxy = maxy + 0.5. Create a GeoDataFrame for the outer boundary as a single rectangular polygon using shapely.geometry.box(expanded_minx, expanded_miny, expanded_maxx, expanded_maxy) with CRS EPSG:4326. Save the Florida state geometry as FloridaState.geojson, the Miami-Dade county geometry as MiamiDadeCounty.geojson, and the expanded bounding box polygon as OuterBoundary.geojson. Also save the four bounding box coordinate values to a plain text file named BoundingBox.txt with exactly four lines in this order: expanded_minx, expanded_miny, expanded_maxx, expanded_maxy (one float per line). Upload all four files — FloridaState.geojson, MiamiDadeCounty.geojson, OuterBoundary.geojson, and BoundingBox.txt — to the S3 data store under the folder MiamiTemperatureHeatmap.

Download BoundingBox.txt from the S3 data store folder MiamiTemperatureHeatmap. Read the four lines to get expanded_minx (line 1), expanded_miny (line 2), expanded_maxx (line 3), expanded_maxy (line 4) as floats. These represent the expanded bounding box around Miami-Dade County, Florida. Download the NOAA GHCND station inventory from https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/pub/data/ghcn/daily/ghcnd-inventory.txt. This file is a fixed-width text file with columns: STATION (characters 1-11), LATITUDE (characters 13-20), LONGITUDE (characters 22-30), ELEMENT (characters 32-35), FIRSTYEAR (characters 37-40), LASTYEAR (characters 42-45). Parse all rows. Keep only rows where ELEMENT is either 'TMAX' or 'TMIN'. Keep only rows where LASTYEAR is greater than or equal to the current calendar year (e.g., 2024 or 2025). From those filtered rows, keep only stations where LATITUDE is between expanded_miny and expanded_maxy (inclusive) and LONGITUDE is between expanded_minx and expanded_maxx (inclusive). A station qualifies only if it has BOTH a TMAX record AND a TMIN record passing all filters. Deduplicate so each qualifying station appears once. Build a GeoDataFrame with columns STATION (string station ID), LATITUDE (float), LONGITUDE (float), and geometry (shapely Point(LONGITUDE, LATITUDE)) with CRS EPSG:4326. Save this as Stations.geojson and upload it to the S3 data store under the folder MiamiTemperatureHeatmap.

Download Stations.geojson from the S3 data store folder MiamiTemperatureHeatmap. Load it as a GeoDataFrame; it has columns STATION, LATITUDE, LONGITUDE, and geometry. Determine the target week as follows: take today's date (the date when this action runs), subtract 28 days to get an anchor date, then find the most recent Monday on or before that anchor date (use Python: anchor - timedelta(days=anchor.weekday()) to get the Monday). The week start is that Monday and the week end is that Monday plus 6 days (the following Sunday). For each station in Stations.geojson, download its full daily CSV record from the URL https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/data/global-historical-climatology-network-daily/access/<STATION_ID>.csv where <STATION_ID> is the value in the STATION column. The CSV has a header row; relevant columns are DATE (format YYYY-MM-DD), TMAX, and TMIN. Filter the CSV rows to only those where DATE falls within the target week (week_start <= DATE <= week_end, inclusive). Drop any rows where TMAX or TMIN is missing or NaN. If no valid rows remain for a station after filtering, skip that station entirely. For stations with valid data, compute the mean of TMAX across all remaining rows and the mean of TMIN across all remaining rows. Divide both means by 10.0 to convert from tenths of a degree Celsius to whole degrees Celsius. Collect all qualifying stations into a single GeoDataFrame with columns: STATION (string), LATITUDE (float), LONGITUDE (float), TMIN (float, in Celsius), TMAX (float, in Celsius), and geometry (Point). Also save a plain text file named TargetWeek.txt with exactly two lines: line 1 is the week start date in YYYY-MM-DD format, line 2 is the week end date in YYYY-MM-DD format. Save the GeoDataFrame as TemperatureData.geojson. Upload both TemperatureData.geojson and TargetWeek.txt to the S3 data store under the folder MiamiTemperatureHeatmap.

Download the following files from the S3 data store folder MiamiTemperatureHeatmap: OuterBoundary.geojson, TemperatureData.geojson, FloridaState.geojson, MiamiDadeCounty.geojson, BoundingBox.txt, and TargetWeek.txt. Load BoundingBox.txt: line 1 is expanded_minx, line 2 is expanded_miny, line 3 is expanded_maxx, line 4 is expanded_maxy (all floats). Load TargetWeek.txt: line 1 is week_start (YYYY-MM-DD), line 2 is week_end (YYYY-MM-DD). Load TemperatureData.geojson as a GeoDataFrame with columns STATION, LATITUDE, LONGITUDE, TMIN, TMAX, and geometry. Load FloridaState.geojson and MiamiDadeCounty.geojson as GeoDataFrames. Build a 100x100 interpolation grid: create 100 evenly spaced longitude values from expanded_minx to expanded_maxx and 100 evenly spaced latitude values from expanded_miny to expanded_maxy using numpy.linspace; use numpy.meshgrid to create grid arrays grid_lon and grid_lat. Extract station coordinates as an array of (LONGITUDE, LATITUDE) pairs and extract TMIN values and TMAX values as separate arrays. Use scipy.interpolate.griddata with method='cubic' to interpolate TMIN values onto the grid (points=station_coords, values=tmin_values, xi=(grid_lon, grid_lat), method='cubic') and similarly for TMAX. Create a matplotlib figure with two side-by-side subplots using figsize=(18, 8). For the left subplot (TMIN): use ax.contourf with the grid_lon, grid_lat, and interpolated TMIN grid, 20 levels, colormap 'Blues_r', and alpha=0.8. Overlay station scatter points using ax.scatter with x=LONGITUDE, y=LATITUDE, c=TMIN values, cmap='Blues_r', edgecolors='black', zorder=5. Plot the FloridaState boundary using its geometry with color='black', linewidth=1. Plot the MiamiDadeCounty boundary using its geometry with color='black', linewidth=1.5. Set x-axis limits to (expanded_minx, expanded_maxx) and y-axis limits to (expanded_miny, expanded_maxy). Set x-ticks and y-ticks every 0.5 degrees within those ranges. Label x-axis 'Longitude' and y-axis 'Latitude'. Add a colorbar labeled 'Temperature (C)'. Title the subplot 'Minimum Temperature Heatmap (C)'. For the right subplot (TMAX): use ax.contourf with the grid_lon, grid_lat, and interpolated TMAX grid, 20 levels, colormap 'Reds', and alpha=0.8. Overlay station scatter points using ax.scatter with x=LONGITUDE, y=LATITUDE, c=TMAX values, cmap='Reds', edgecolors='black', zorder=5. Plot the FloridaState boundary with color='black', linewidth=1. Plot the MiamiDadeCounty boundary with color='black', linewidth=1.5. Set the same axis limits, tick spacing, and axis labels as the left subplot. Add a colorbar labeled 'Temperature (C)'. Title the subplot 'Maximum Temperature Heatmap (C)'. Parse week_start to get a datetime object and format it as '<weekday>, <month> <day>, <year>' (e.g., 'Monday, June 2, 2025'). Parse week_end similarly. Set the figure supertitle to 'Temperature Heatmap for Miami-Dade County for week of <week_start_formatted> to <week_end_formatted>' using plt.suptitle with fontsize=14. Call plt.tight_layout(). Save the figure as TemperatureHeatmap.png at 150 DPI. Upload TemperatureHeatmap.png to the S3 data store under the folder MiamiTemperatureHeatmap.
